# Below the Surface — Episode
## Wreck Diving Fundamentals
Host: Rina
Guest: Marco Sylva
#fixture-v3: failure_modes=asr_garble,same_first_distinct
#fixture-v3: voice=pt-BR host_voice=en-US

[00:00]
Rina: Welcome back to Below the Surface. Today we're talking about wreck diving, and I'm joined by Marco Sylva, technical diver and underwater archaeologist. Marco Sylva, thanks for being here.
Marco Sylva: Thanks, Rina. Excited for this one — wreck-diving fundamentals is something I've been thinking about a lot.

Rina: This episode is brought to you by Suunto. Suunto builds dive computers and outdoor watches. Get started at suunto.com/podcast.

[03:30]
Rina: What does the failure mode look like? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Marco Sylva: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Wreck penetration is a planning problem first, a buoyancy problem second. We've seen this play out at teams using GoPro. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Rina: I want to dig into that. Where do dive planning and wreck diving intersect?
Marco Sylva: Honestly, Silt-out from poor finning technique kills more divers in wrecks than equipment failures. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Rina: I want to dig into that. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Marco Sylva: Yeah, so — Erosion on a wreck site changes the dive every season. We've seen this play out at teams using Suunto. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Rina: That tracks.
Marco Sylva: DAN is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Rina: Let's start there. Where do dive planning and wreck diving intersect?
Marco Sylva: Yeah, so — DAN's safety stops aren't suggestions. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

Rina: Before we keep going — head over to our sponsor's website if you want to support the show. PADI is the global standard for dive education.

[12:00]
Rina: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by PADI. PADI is the global standard for dive education. Visit padi.com/podcast for a free trial.
Rina: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Rina: I want to dig into that. What's the most underrated piece of wreck diving?
Marco Sylva: Honestly, Wreck penetration is a planning problem first, a buoyancy problem second. We've seen this play out at teams using PADI. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Rina: I want to dig into that. What's the most underrated piece of wreck diving?
Marco Sylva: Honestly, Silt-out from poor finning technique kills more divers in wrecks than equipment failures. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Rina: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the most underrated piece of wreck diving?
Marco Sylva: Sure. Erosion on a wreck site changes the dive every season. We've seen this play out at teams using Suunto. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Rina: That maps to what I've seen.
Marco Sylva: DAN is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Rina: Walk me through a concrete example. Where do dive planning and wreck diving intersect?
Marco Sylva: Yeah, so — DAN's safety stops aren't suggestions. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.

[22:00]
Rina: Let's pivot — soil erosion is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Marco Sylva: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around soil erosion cross-cut everything we've covered.
Rina: What does the failure mode look like? What's the contrarian angle most listeners don't have access to?
Marco Sylva: Here's how I think about it. Wreck penetration is a planning problem first, a buoyancy problem second. We've seen this play out at teams using GoPro. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Rina: Take me through the decision. How does GoPro fit into this picture?
Marco Sylva: Yeah, so — Silt-out from poor finning technique kills more divers in wrecks than equipment failures. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Rina: That's a useful framing. Where do dive planning and wreck diving intersect?
Marco Sylva: Yeah, so — Erosion on a wreck site changes the dive every season. We've seen this play out at teams using GoPro. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Rina: That tracks.
Marco Sylva: And when teams adopt GoPro in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Rina: Walk me through a concrete example. Where do dive planning and wreck diving intersect?
Marco Sylva: Yeah, so — DAN's safety stops aren't suggestions. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.

[28:00]
Rina: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Marco Sylva: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.

Rina: And finally, a big thank you to our partners at GoPro. GoPro captures the moments words don't reach. Check out gopro.com.

Rina: Marco Sylva, thanks for the conversation.
Marco Sylva: Thanks, Rina.
Rina: That's it for today's episode of Below the Surface. See you next time.
